summary: My father, Eugene Talmadge, was first elected Commissioner of Agriculture in 1926 after several unsuccessful runs for local office and the legislature. He was a maverick who defied the political machine in Telfair County, and once he ran for statewide office, he gained loyal support until his death. I vividly remember his debates with J.J. Brown in 1926, which helped him win the Commissioner race. He later became governor, carrying most counties except a few like Fulton, DeKalb, and Clarke after his 1932 race against Abit Nix. He was a forceful man, both in politics and on the farm, and many myths about him have been repeated over time, though not all are true.

Actually, my father was elected Commissioner of Agriculture in 1926, not Secretary. That was his first statewide office after several attempts at local and legislative positions. The Commissioner of Agriculture role was significant because it marked the start of his successful political career, which later led him to become governor. I was about twelve or thirteen then and actively involved in his campaign, including attending his debates and helping with campaign efforts.

My father ran twice for the Georgia House of Representatives but was defeated both times. The local political machine in Telfair County didn't want him in the legislature because he wouldn't take orders. Despite these early setbacks, he persevered and eventually won statewide office. These early defeats shaped his maverick political style and determination.

My father also ran once for the State Senate but was defeated, again due to the political machine in Telfair County opposing him. This was before he gained statewide recognition. These early losses didn't discourage him; instead, they motivated him to run for Commissioner of Agriculture and later governor, where he found greater success.

Telfair County was where my father lived and ran for local office early in his career. The political machine there was strong and opposed him because he wouldn't follow their orders. This opposition kept him out of the legislature initially, but once he ran for statewide office, the county's political forces supported him loyally. It was also the area where our family farm was located, and where I spent much of my childhood.

J.J. Brown was a powerful political figure in Georgia when my father ran for Commissioner of Agriculture in 1926. He had a vast political machine and was considered unbeatable. My father debated him three times—in McRae, Elberton, and Dawson—and my father won those debates decisively. I remember attending the first debate in McRae and even helped by bringing watermelons for the crowd.

McRae was my father's hometown and the site of the first debate against J.J. Brown during the 1926 Commissioner of Agriculture race. I was there as a boy and helped by bringing watermelons to feed the crowd. It was a significant place in my father's early political career and where he first gained attention as a forceful debater.

Elberton was J.J. Brown's hometown and the location of the second debate between my father and Brown in 1926. This debate was part of a series that helped my father win the Commissioner of Agriculture race. It was a key stop in that campaign and showed my father's ability to challenge established political powers.

Dawson, Georgia served as neutral territory for the third debate between my father and J.J. Brown in 1926. This debate was crucial in showcasing my father's debating skills and helped him secure the Commissioner of Agriculture position. It was part of a memorable series of debates that marked the start of his statewide political career.

I attended the University of Georgia and had just finished my freshman year there in 1932 when my father ran for governor. While at the university, I helped with his campaign by advertising his speeches in towns around the state. My father and I both had connections to the university; he and my grandfather were students there, and it was a place where he was well known and respected.

Cedartown was one of the towns where I advertised my father's speeches during his 1932 gubernatorial campaign. I remember going into a meat market there and facing some hostility, but I kept delivering circulars. It was part of my early political involvement and gave me a firsthand look at the challenges of campaigning.

Turner County, near Sycamore, was the site of my first political speech in 1934 during my father's reelection campaign for governor. A friend from college organized a big rally there with hundreds of people attending. It was also where I later met my wife, Betty. My father carried Turner County overwhelmingly in that election.

Sycamore is a small town near where my first political speech took place in Turner County in 1934. A college friend from Sycamore helped organize a large rally for my father, which was a significant moment for me as a young man entering politics. It was an exciting time and marked the start of my own political career.

Fulton County was one of the few counties my father did not carry in his 1932 gubernatorial race, though he did carry it in a later race. It was part of the urban areas where he had mixed success, unlike many rural counties where he was very strong. The political dynamics there were different from the rural counties he dominated.

DeKalb County was another county my father never carried in his races for governor. It was part of the urban Atlanta area where his political support was weaker. Despite his statewide popularity, DeKalb remained a challenging area for him politically.

Clarke County was initially very supportive of my father because he and my grandfather had been students at the University of Georgia and had many relatives there. However, after my father defeated Abit Nix, a favorite son of Clarke County, in the 1932 governor's race, he never carried Clarke County again. Interestingly, I have carried Clarke County in every race I have run, including for the U.S. Senate.

Abit Nix was a prominent political figure from Clarke County and my father's opponent in the 1932 governor's race. My father defeated him, which caused Clarke County to stop supporting my father thereafter. Later, Abit Nix and I became friends, and he even introduced me when I ran for the U.S. Senate, a race in which I carried Clarke County.

Chattooga County was where my father made his first speech after being nominated for governor in 1932, at a fair in Summerville. It was there that he made a statement about not carrying counties where streetcars ran, which later became a political legend, though it was a misinterpretation. This county marked an important early moment in his gubernatorial campaign.

Summerville, in Chattooga County, was the site of my father's first speech after his 1932 nomination for governor. It was at a fair there that he made a remark about not carrying counties with streetcars, which became a famous but inaccurate legend. Summerville holds a place in the history of my father's campaign.

Richmond County was one of the counties with streetcars that my father carried most of the time in his gubernatorial races. This fact contradicts the legend that he avoided counties with streetcars. Richmond County was part of the urban support base that helped him win statewide.

Chatham County was another urban county with streetcars that my father carried most of the time during his campaigns. It was part of the broader geographic support he had across Georgia, despite some myths about his political base. Chatham County's support was important in his statewide success.

Muscogee County, like Richmond and Chatham, had streetcars and was carried by my father most of the time in his gubernatorial races. This further disproves the legend that he avoided counties with streetcars. Muscogee County was part of the diverse coalition that supported him.

There is a famous quote often attributed to my father that 'The farmer has only three friends: God Almighty, Sears Roebuck and Gene Talmadge.' However, my father never actually said that. It was a statement made by someone introducing him once, and it got wrongly attributed to him over time. This is just one example of many myths surrounding my father's legacy.

Eugene Cook was the Attorney General during my early political career, and there is a humorous story that I wrote him asking if I could run for a full term as governor after serving an unexpired term, ending with a question about running for Attorney General if ineligible. That story is not true, but it has been told many times. It shows how political myths can develop around public figures.
